g.data-narration: Welcome to Coprocessor Instructions. We will explore how specialized processors enhance microcontroller capabilities.
A coprocessor is a secondary processor that works alongside the main CPU to handle specialized computational tasks efficiently.
g.text: A secondary processor that works alongside the main CPU to handle specialized tasks like
floating-point arithmetic, signal processing, and graphics operations.

g.data-narration: ARM supports up to 16 coprocessors (CP0-CP15), each serving specific purposes from user-defined to system control.
g.text: ARM supports up to 16 coprocessors (CP0 - CP15):

g.data-narration: Three main coprocessor types exist in microcontrollers: Floating-Point Units for math, DSPs for signal processing, and dedicated math coprocessors.
ARM provides four main coprocessor instruction types: CDP for operations, LDC/STC for data transfer, and MRC/MCR for register transfer.
g.text: ARM provides four main instruction types for coprocessor interaction:

g.data-narration: CDP performs operations directly within the coprocessor without involving main processor registers.
g.text: Performs operation within coprocessor without main CPU registers:

g.data-narration: LDC and STC instructions transfer data between memory and coprocessor registers for loading and storing values.
g.text: Transfer data between memory and coprocessor registers:
VLDR S0, [R0] ; Load float from address in R0

g.data-narration: MRC transfers data from coprocessor to ARM register, while MCR transfers from ARM register to coprocessor.
g.text: Move data between ARM registers and coprocessor registers:
MRC - Move from Coprocessor to Register
MCR - Move from Register to Coprocessor

g.data-narration: The VFP instruction set handles floating-point operations including arithmetic, load/store, and conversion instructions.
VFP provides S-registers for 32-bit single precision, D-registers for 64-bit double precision, and Q-registers for 128-bit SIMD operations.
In Cortex-M processors, the FPU must be explicitly enabled through the CPACR register before use.
g.text: In Cortex-M4/M7, the FPU must be explicitly enabled via CPACR (Coprocessor Access Control Register):
LDR r1, [r0] / ORR r1, r1, #0x00F00000 / STR r1, [r0] ; Enable CP10/CP11

g.data-narration: This example demonstrates loading two floats from memory, adding them with VADD, and storing the result.
g.text: Problem: Add two floats at addresses 0x20000000 and 0x20000004, store result at 0x20000008

g.data-narration: SIMD instructions process multiple data elements in parallel. NEON can add four floats simultaneously in a single instruction.
g.text: Single Instruction Multiple Data (SIMD) processes multiple values in parallel:
VADD.F32 q2, q0, q1 ; 4 parallel adds

g.data-narration: Key points: ARM supports 16 coprocessors, MRC transfers from coprocessor to ARM, MCR does the opposite, and SIMD enables parallel processing.
Remember: enable FPU in Cortex-M, distinguish MRC from MCR correctly, use S-registers for single precision floats.
g.text: Remember CP15 is system control coprocessor
MRC = Move from Coprocessor to ARM register
MCR = Move from ARM register to Coprocessor
Enable FPU in Cortex-M before using

g.data-narration: Practice with coprocessor instructions to master embedded systems programming. Understanding these concepts is essential for ARM-based microcontroller development.
g.text: Keep Practicing! Coprocessors are fundamental to efficient embedded systems.
